---
title: "Terminologies and Semantic Interoperability"
lang: en-GB
---

## The Problem of Free Text {#sec-free-text}

### Ambiguity in clinical information

Free-text clinical documentation is, historically, the dominant form
of record-keeping in health systems. However, free text introduces a
structural problem: semantic ambiguity. The same clinical concept can
be expressed in different ways by different professionals, in different
contexts, making automatic aggregation and comparison of information
impossible [@BensonGrieve2021].

A paradigmatic example: the term "high tension" can be interpreted as
systemic arterial hypertension, emotional tension or tension headache,
depending on the professional and the clinical context. Three distinct
interpretations of the same free-text term, with completely different
diagnostic and therapeutic implications.

The practical consequences of this ambiguity are well documented:
communication errors between professionals; inability to aggregate
data for statistics or clinical research; failure in continuity of care
between institutions; and, ultimately, systems that exchange data but
do not share meaning.

### From ambiguity to structured coding

The solution to the ambiguity problem lies in replacing free text with
**structured coding**: the assignment of standardised codes to clinical
concepts, so that the same concept always has the same code, regardless
of who records it or where it is recorded. Figure 3.1 illustrates this
contrast.

```{=html}
<!--
  Visual contrast: free text (red/warning) vs coded (green/ok).
  Two cards side by side with transformation arrow.
-->
<div style="display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;gap:1rem;
            align-items:stretch;margin:2rem 0;justify-content:center;">

  <!-- Free text -->
  <div style="flex:1;min-width:200px;max-width:300px;
              background:#fff5f5;border:2px solid #c0392b;
              border-radius:8px;padding:1.2rem;box-sizing:border-box;">
    <div style="display:flex;align-items:center;gap:.5rem;margin-bottom:.6rem;">
      <span style="font-size:1.1rem;">⚠️</span>
      <span style="font-weight:700;font-size:.85rem;color:#c0392b;">
        Anamnesis (free text)
      </span>
    </div>
    <p style="font-size:.85rem;color:#444;font-style:italic;margin:0;">
      "Patient with high blood pressure, elevated sugar,
       complains of chest pain since yesterday."
    </p>
    <p style="font-size:.75rem;color:#888;margin:.6rem 0 0;">
      Ambiguous · not searchable · not comparable
    </p>
  </div>

  <!-- arrow -->
  <div style="display:flex;align-items:center;font-size:1.6rem;
              color:#0d7c8c;padding:0 .5rem;">→</div>

  <!-- Coded -->
  <div style="flex:1;min-width:200px;max-width:300px;
              background:#f0fafa;border:2px solid #0d7c8c;
              border-radius:8px;padding:1.2rem;box-sizing:border-box;">
    <div style="display:flex;align-items:center;gap:.5rem;margin-bottom:.6rem;">
      <span style="font-size:1.1rem;">✅</span>
      <span style="font-weight:700;font-size:.85rem;color:#0d7c8c;">
        Coded (SNOMED CT)
      </span>
    </div>
    <div style="font-size:.85rem;line-height:1.5;color:#0a3d4a;">
      <p style="margin:0 0 .6rem 0;"><strong>38341003:</strong><br>Hypertension</p>
      <p style="margin:0 0 .6rem 0;"><strong>44054006:</strong><br>Diabetes type 2</p>
      <p style="margin:0;"><strong>29857009:</strong><br>Chest pain</p>
    </div>
    <p style="font-size:.75rem;color:#0d7c8c;margin:.6rem 0 0;font-weight:600;">
      Precise · searchable · interoperable
    </p>
  </div>
</div>
```

<p style="text-align:center;font-size:14px;color:#475569;margin-top:.5rem;">
Figure 3.1 — Contrast between free-text recording and structured coding (SNOMED CT)
</p>

## Key Concepts {#sec-key-concepts}

### Technical versus semantic interoperability

The distinction between technical and semantic interoperability is
central to Medical Informatics and must be clarified precisely:

**Technical interoperability** refers to the ability of two systems to
exchange data at the level of transport, format and protocol. It
guarantees that bytes reach the destination; it does not guarantee that
meaning is preserved. Example: sending an HL7 v2 message between two
systems.

**Semantic interoperability** refers to the ability of two systems to
share the meaning of the data exchanged. It guarantees that the receiver
interprets the message content in the same way as the sender. Example:
both systems interpret code `386661006` as fever, because both use
SNOMED CT [@BensonGrieve2021].

### Terminology, classification and ontology

Three related but distinct concepts structure the clinical coding
ecosystem:

A **terminology** is a controlled, detailed vocabulary that codes
clinical data entry. It admits multiple synonyms per concept and aims
for granular clinical documentation. SNOMED CT is the reference
example.

A **classification** groups concepts into fixed categories for
statistical output and reporting purposes. Each concept occupies a
unique place in the hierarchical tree. ICD and ICF are the paradigmatic
examples.

An **ontology** is a formal knowledge model that defines concepts and
their logical relationships, enabling automatic inference. SNOMED CT
is simultaneously a terminology and an ontology: its relationship
structure enables computational reasoning over concepts.

## SNOMED CT {#sec-snomed}

### What is SNOMED CT?

SNOMED CT (*Systematized Nomenclature of Medicine — Clinical Terms*)
is the most comprehensive clinical terminology and ontology system in
the world. Maintained by SNOMED International, it is in use in more
than 40 countries and constitutes the reference terminology for coding
clinical data entry in modern EHRs [@SNOMED2024].

Its scope is organised across three dimensions: terminology (controlled
clinical vocabulary with more than 350,000 concepts), interoperability
(standardisation of clinical language between institutions and systems)
and ontological structure (poly-hierarchical graph of concepts and
relationships).

### The three fundamental components

SNOMED CT is organised around three components that work together to
represent clinical knowledge precisely and computably:

**Concepts** — atomic units of clinical meaning, each identified by a
unique and permanent numeric *Concept ID*. Examples: `38341003` =
Hypertension; `73211009` = Diabetes mellitus; `386661006` = Fever.

**Descriptions** — human-readable terms associated with each concept:
the *Fully Specified Name* (FSN), which specifies the concept
unambiguously; the *Preferred Term*, the preferred name in the local
language; and synonyms, allowing multiple designations for the same
concept.

**Relationships** — typed links between concepts that form the
ontological graph. The main types are: `is-a` (taxonomic hierarchy),
`finding-site` (anatomical location), and `causative-agent` (causative
agent). The poly-hierarchy allows a concept to have multiple parents,
reflecting the complexity of clinical reality.

### Example: Bacterial Pneumonia

The concept of bacterial pneumonia illustrates the expressive richness
of SNOMED CT through its relationships. The formal expression of the
concept is:

```{=html}
<!--
  SNOMED CT formal expression — presented as independent short lines.
-->
<div style="background:#e8f7f8;border-left:4px solid #0d7c8c;
            border-radius:4px;padding:.8rem 1.2rem;margin:1rem 0;
            font-family:monospace;font-size:.8rem;line-height:1.6;
            color:#0a3d4a;">
  <div><strong>53084003</strong> | Bacterial pneumonia (disorder)</div>
  <div style="padding-left:1.2rem;color:#0d7c8c;">├─ finding-site:
    <strong>39607008</strong> | Lung structure</div>
  <div style="padding-left:1.2rem;color:#0d7c8c;">└─ causative-agent:
    <strong>56415008</strong> | Genus Streptococcus</div>
</div>
```

Compact form (ECL syntax — *Expression Constraint Language*):

```{=html}
<div style="background:#e8f7f8;border-left:4px solid #1ab3a6;
            border-radius:4px;padding:.7rem 1.2rem;margin:1rem 0;
            font-family:monospace;font-size:.85rem;color:#0a3d4a;
            line-height:1.4;white-space:nowrap;overflow-x:auto;">
  53084003:363698007=39607008,246075003=9861002
</div>
```

This example demonstrates that SNOMED CT does not merely name concepts:
it expresses complex clinical relationships in a computable form,
enabling automatic reasoning over medical knowledge. Figure 3.2
represents this hierarchy graphically.

```{=html}
<!--
  SNOMED CT hierarchy for Bacterial Pneumonia.
  Vertical tree: Clinical finding → Disorder → Respiratory disorder →
  Pneumonia → Bacterial pneumonia, with finding-site and causative-agent branches.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto;margin:2rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 580 350" width="560" height="340"
     xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     aria-label="SNOMED CT hierarchy — Bacterial Pneumonia">

  <!-- is-a vertical main line -->
  <line x1="290" y1="36" x2="290" y2="82"
        stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <line x1="290" y1="104" x2="290" y2="148"
        stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <line x1="290" y1="170" x2="290" y2="214"
        stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <line x1="290" y1="236" x2="290" y2="268"
        stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>

  <!-- Clinical finding (root) -->
  <rect x="180" y="8" width="220" height="28" rx="5"
        fill="#e8f7f8" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="290" y="27" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        font-weight="600" fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">
    Clinical finding [SNOMED root]</text>

  <!-- Disorder -->
  <rect x="180" y="82" width="220" height="24" rx="5"
        fill="#e8f7f8" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="290" y="99" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        font-weight="600" fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">Disorder</text>

  <!-- Respiratory disorder -->
  <rect x="180" y="148" width="220" height="24" rx="5"
        fill="#e8f7f8" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="290" y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        font-weight="600" fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">Respiratory disorder</text>

  <!-- Pneumonia -->
  <rect x="180" y="214" width="220" height="24" rx="5"
        fill="#cceef2" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.3"/>
  <text x="290" y="231" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        font-weight="600" fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">Pneumonia [233604007]</text>

  <!-- Bacterial pneumonia (highlight) -->
  <rect x="175" y="268" width="230" height="28" rx="6"
        fill="#0d7c8c" stroke="#0a3d4a" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="290" y="287" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        font-weight="700" fill="#fff" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">
    Bacterial pneumonia [53084003]</text>

  <!-- finding-site branch → Lung -->
  <line x1="250" y1="282" x2="110" y2="252"
        stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <rect x="28" y="234" width="115" height="36" rx="5"
        fill="#d6f2ef" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="85" y="249" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">finding-site →</text>
  <text x="85" y="263" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        font-weight="600" fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">Lung [39607008]</text>

  <!-- causative-agent branch → Streptococcus -->
  <line x1="330" y1="282" x2="454" y2="252"
        stroke="#f39c12" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <rect x="400" y="234" width="158" height="36" rx="5"
        fill="#fef9ec" stroke="#f39c12" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="479" y="249" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">causative-agent →</text>
  <text x="479" y="263" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        font-weight="600" fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">Genus Streptococcus</text>

  <!-- relationship type legend -->
  <line x1="22" y1="310" x2="46" y2="310"
        stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <text x="50" y="314" font-size="11" fill="#555"
        font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">is-a</text>
  <line x1="90" y1="310" x2="114" y2="310"
        stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="118" y="314" font-size="11" fill="#555"
        font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">finding-site</text>
  <line x1="190" y1="310" x2="214" y2="310"
        stroke="#f39c12" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="218" y="314" font-size="11" fill="#555"
        font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">causative-agent</text>
</svg>
</div>

<p style="text-align:center;font-size:14px;color:#475569;margin-top:.5rem;">
Figure 3.2 — SNOMED CT hierarchy for the concept of Bacterial Pneumonia (53084003), with <em>is-a</em>, <em>finding-site</em> and <em>causative-agent</em> relationships
</p>
```

## ICD — International Classification of Diseases {#sec-icd}

### Characterisation and purpose

The ICD (*International Classification of Diseases and Related Health
Problems*) is a classification system maintained by the World Health
Organisation (WHO) and adopted by 194 member states. The version
currently in force is ICD-11, adopted in 2022, with more than 55,000
codes organised across 26 chapters [@WHO_ICD11].

The purpose of the ICD is fundamentally distinct from SNOMED CT: while
the latter serves clinical data entry with high granularity, the ICD
serves **output** — epidemiological reporting, health statistics and
hospital billing. It is a classification, not a terminology: it
organises diseases into fixed categories according to a mono-axial
hierarchy.

### Hierarchical structure

The ICD structure is organised at three levels: chapter, block and
category. The example of the respiratory chapter illustrates this
organisation:

```{=html}
<!--
  ICD-11 hierarchical tree — respiratory chapter.
-->
<div style="background:#fef9ec;border-left:4px solid #f39c12;
            border-radius:4px;padding:1rem 1.2rem;margin:1rem 0;
            font-family:monospace;font-size:.85rem;line-height:1.9;
            color:#0a3d4a;overflow-wrap:break-word;">
  <div>Chapter 12 — Diseases of the respiratory system</div>
  <div style="padding-left:1.2rem;">└── Block CA00–CA08 — Acute respiratory infections</div>
  <div style="padding-left:2.4rem;">├── CA40 — Common cold</div>
  <div style="padding-left:3.6rem;">├── CA40.0 — Acute rhinitis</div>
  <div style="padding-left:3.6rem;">└── CA40.Z — Cold, unspecified</div>
  <div style="padding-left:2.4rem;">└── BA00 — Bacterial pneumonia</div>
</div>
```

Some frequently used reference codes:

| ICD-11 Code | Disease |
|---|---|
| `CA40` | Common cold |
| `BA00` | Bacterial pneumonia |
| `5A11` | Diabetes mellitus type 2 |
| `8B11` | Ischaemic stroke |
| `BA41` | Acute myocardial infarction |

: Examples of ICD-11 codes {#tbl-icd}

### SNOMED CT versus ICD — complementarity

SNOMED CT and ICD are not competitors: they are complementary, each
suited to its purpose. SNOMED CT encodes clinical detail at the point
of care; ICD classifies for reporting and billing. SNOMED International
maintains official maps between the two systems, allowing a SNOMED CT
concept to be automatically translated into the corresponding ICD code.

| Dimension | SNOMED CT | ICD |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Clinical terminology | Statistical classification |
| **Function** | Input — clinical recording | Output — reporting and billing |
| **Granularity** | Very high (>350,000) | Medium (~55,000) |
| **Structure** | Poly-hierarchical graph | Mono-hierarchical tree |
| **Maintained by** | SNOMED International | WHO |

: SNOMED CT vs ICD — comparison {#tbl-snomed-icd}

## LOINC — Laboratory Observations {#sec-loinc}

### Definition and scope

LOINC (*Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes*) is a
terminology maintained by the Regenstrief Institute that provides a
universal language for identifying laboratory measurements, clinical
observations and health documents [@McDonald2003]. Its scope is
organised across three domains: clinical laboratory (full blood count,
glucose, tumour markers); physiological observations (vital signs,
assessment scales); and documents (discharge notes, reports).

### The anatomy of a LOINC code — the six axes

Each LOINC code is an opaque identifier (e.g. `2345-7`), whose meaning
is entirely encoded in its formal name. This name results from the
combination of six axes that describe, precisely, all relevant aspects
of a measurement:

| Axis | Name | Question | Example (blood glucose) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Component | What? | Glucose |
| 2 | Property | Which quantity? | MCnc (mass/volume) |
| 3 | Timing | When? | Pt (point in time) |
| 4 | System | Where? | Ser/Plas (serum/plasma) |
| 5 | Scale | How? | Qn (quantitative) |
| 6 | Method | With what? | (optional) Enzymatic |

: The six axes of a LOINC code — blood glucose example {#tbl-loinc}

Code `2345-7` corresponds to: *Glucose \[Mass/volume\] in Serum or
Plasma*. This unique identifier ensures that two laboratories from
different institutions report blood glucose in a comparable way,
regardless of their internal systems. Figure 3.3 visualises the six
axes for this concrete example.

```{=html}
<!--
  LOINC six axes visualised as horizontal stacked cards.
  Concrete example: Glucose in serum = 95 mg/dL, point-in-time collection.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto;margin:2rem 0;">
<div style="display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;gap:3px;justify-content:center;">

  <!-- context card -->
  <div style="background:#0a3d4a;color:#fff;border-radius:6px;
              padding:.7rem .9rem;min-width:120px;
              display:flex;flex-direction:column;justify-content:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.7rem;opacity:.7;margin-bottom:.3rem;">Real measurement</div>
    <div style="font-size:.8rem;font-weight:600;line-height:1.4;">
      Glucose in serum<br>95 mg/dL<br>fasting
    </div>
    <div style="margin-top:.6rem;font-size:.75rem;font-weight:700;
                color:#1ab3a6;">Code: 2345-7</div>
  </div>

  <!-- axis 1 Component -->
  <div style="background:#e8f7f8;border:1.5px solid #0d7c8c;
              padding:.6rem .7rem;min-width:82px;
              display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;
              text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.65rem;color:#0d7c8c;font-weight:700;
                text-transform:uppercase;margin-bottom:.3rem;">1 Component</div>
    <div style="font-size:.85rem;font-weight:700;color:#0a3d4a;">Glucose</div>
    <div style="font-size:.68rem;color:#666;margin-top:.2rem;">What?</div>
  </div>

  <!-- axis 2 Property -->
  <div style="background:#e8f7f8;border:1.5px solid #0d7c8c;
              padding:.6rem .7rem;min-width:82px;
              display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;
              text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.65rem;color:#0d7c8c;font-weight:700;
                text-transform:uppercase;margin-bottom:.3rem;">2 Property</div>
    <div style="font-size:.85rem;font-weight:700;color:#0a3d4a;">MCnc</div>
    <div style="font-size:.68rem;color:#666;margin-top:.2rem;">Which quantity?</div>
  </div>

  <!-- axis 3 Timing -->
  <div style="background:#e8f7f8;border:1.5px solid #0d7c8c;
              padding:.6rem .7rem;min-width:72px;
              display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;
              text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.65rem;color:#0d7c8c;font-weight:700;
                text-transform:uppercase;margin-bottom:.3rem;">3 Timing</div>
    <div style="font-size:.85rem;font-weight:700;color:#0a3d4a;">Pt</div>
    <div style="font-size:.68rem;color:#666;margin-top:.2rem;">When?</div>
  </div>

  <!-- axis 4 System -->
  <div style="background:#e8f7f8;border:1.5px solid #0d7c8c;
              padding:.6rem .7rem;min-width:82px;
              display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;
              text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.65rem;color:#0d7c8c;font-weight:700;
                text-transform:uppercase;margin-bottom:.3rem;">4 System</div>
    <div style="font-size:.85rem;font-weight:700;color:#0a3d4a;">Ser/Plas</div>
    <div style="font-size:.68rem;color:#666;margin-top:.2rem;">Where?</div>
  </div>

  <!-- axis 5 Scale -->
  <div style="background:#e8f7f8;border:1.5px solid #0d7c8c;
              padding:.6rem .7rem;min-width:72px;
              display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;
              text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.65rem;color:#0d7c8c;font-weight:700;
                text-transform:uppercase;margin-bottom:.3rem;">5 Scale</div>
    <div style="font-size:.85rem;font-weight:700;color:#0a3d4a;">Qn</div>
    <div style="font-size:.68rem;color:#666;margin-top:.2rem;">How?</div>
  </div>

  <!-- axis 6 Method -->
  <div style="background:#f0fafa;border:1.5px solid #1ab3a6;
              border-radius:6px;
              padding:.6rem .7rem;min-width:82px;
              display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;
              text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.65rem;color:#1ab3a6;font-weight:700;
                text-transform:uppercase;margin-bottom:.3rem;">6 Method</div>
    <div style="font-size:.82rem;font-weight:700;color:#0a3d4a;">Enzymatic</div>
    <div style="font-size:.68rem;color:#666;margin-top:.2rem;">(optional)</div>
  </div>

</div>
</div>

<p style="text-align:center;font-size:14px;color:#475569;margin-top:.5rem;">
Figure 3.3 — The six axes of a LOINC code, illustrated with blood glucose in serum/plasma (code 2345-7)
</p>
```

## ICF — International Classification of Functioning {#sec-icf}

The ICF (*International Classification of Functioning, Disability and
Health*) is a WHO classification that complements the ICD, shifting
the focus from disease to **functioning** and social participation
[@WHO_ICF]. While the ICD answers the question *"what does the patient
have?"*, the ICF answers *"what can the patient do?"*.

This distinction is clinically relevant: two patients with the same
ICD diagnosis may have completely different functioning profiles, with
different implications for rehabilitation, social support and return
to activity.

The ICF adopts a biopsychosocial model, recognising that disability
results from the interaction between the individual's health condition
and contextual factors — both personal and environmental.

## Integration of Terminologies in the Clinical Workflow {#sec-integration}

None of the terminologies described operates in isolation. Effective
semantic interoperability results from their coordinated use throughout
the clinical workflow:

At the time of **clinical recording**, the health professional codes
symptoms, diagnoses and procedures in SNOMED CT, ensuring the semantic
precision of data entry.

At the time of the **laboratory examination**, requests and results are
identified by LOINC codes, ensuring that the same analysis has the same
meaning in any laboratory.

At the time of **reporting and billing**, SNOMED CT codes are
automatically mapped to ICD, through the official maps maintained by
SNOMED International, producing the statistical and billing data
required by health authorities. Figure 3.4 summarises this integrated
workflow.

```{=html}
<!--
  Integrated flow of the three terminologies: SNOMED CT → LOINC → ICD.
  Three boxes in sequence with arrows and context labels.
-->
<div style="display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;
            justify-content:center;gap:.5rem;margin:2rem 0;">

  <div style="flex:1;min-width:140px;max-width:175px;
              background:#e8f7f8;border:2px solid #0d7c8c;
              border-radius:8px;padding:1rem;text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.68rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;
                color:#0d7c8c;letter-spacing:.06em;margin-bottom:.4rem;">
      Clinical Recording</div>
    <div style="font-size:1.05rem;font-weight:800;color:#0a3d4a;">
      SNOMED CT</div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;color:#555;margin-top:.4rem;">
      Symptoms, diagnoses, procedures</div>
  </div>

  <div style="font-size:1.4rem;color:#0d7c8c;">→</div>

  <div style="flex:1;min-width:140px;max-width:175px;
              background:#e8f7f8;border:2px solid #1ab3a6;
              border-radius:8px;padding:1rem;text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.68rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;
                color:#1ab3a6;letter-spacing:.06em;margin-bottom:.4rem;">
      Laboratory</div>
    <div style="font-size:1.05rem;font-weight:800;color:#0a3d4a;">
      LOINC</div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;color:#555;margin-top:.4rem;">
      Tests, observations, results</div>
  </div>

  <div style="font-size:1.4rem;color:#1ab3a6;">→</div>

  <div style="flex:1;min-width:140px;max-width:175px;
              background:#fef9ec;border:2px solid #f39c12;
              border-radius:8px;padding:1rem;text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.68rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;
                color:#e67e22;letter-spacing:.06em;margin-bottom:.4rem;">
      Reporting / Billing</div>
    <div style="font-size:1.05rem;font-weight:800;color:#0a3d4a;">
      ICD</div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;color:#555;margin-top:.4rem;">
      Epidemiology and DRG coding</div>
  </div>

</div>

<p style="text-align:center;font-size:14px;color:#475569;margin-top:.5rem;">
Figure 3.4 — Integrated flow of clinical terminologies: SNOMED CT for recording, LOINC in the laboratory and ICD for reporting and billing
</p>
```

## Summary {#sec-summary-03}

This chapter addressed the problem of semantic ambiguity in clinical
information and the standardised terminologies that resolve it. The
SNOMED CT / LOINC / ICD / ICF ecosystem was presented as a coordinated
set of tools, each with a distinct purpose in the clinical workflow,
and none sufficient on its own.

The following key points are retained:

1. Free text is the main obstacle to semantic interoperability;
   structured coding is its operational alternative.
2. Semantic interoperability is distinct from technical: it is not
   enough for data to reach the destination — they must be correctly
   interpreted.
3. SNOMED CT is the reference clinical entry terminology; ICD is the
   output classification for reporting and billing; LOINC standardises
   laboratory observations; ICF addresses the functioning dimension.
4. The terminologies are complementary and linked through official
   maps: the clinician records in SNOMED CT; the system maps to ICD;
   the manager obtains comparable statistics.

## Consolidation exercises {.unnumbered}

1. Explain, with a concrete example different from those presented in
   the chapter, how free text can introduce clinical ambiguity with
   consequences for patient safety.

2. Distinguish terminology, classification and ontology. For each of
   the three concepts, give an example from the health terminology
   ecosystem and describe its specific purpose.

3. Construct the SNOMED CT expression (in compact notation) for the
   concept of "*Klebsiella pneumoniae* pneumonia", indicating the
   `finding-site` and `causative-agent` attributes with their
   respective codes (search at `browser.ihtsdotools.org` if needed).

4. A hospital laboratory reports a haemoglobin value with LOINC code
   `718-7`. Identify the six axes of this code and explain why the
   use of LOINC guarantees comparability between laboratories from
   different institutions.

5. Consider the complete workflow of a patient diagnosed with bacterial
   pneumonia. For each stage of the workflow (clinical recording,
   laboratory, discharge/billing), indicate which terminology is used
   and the corresponding code.
